perturbation.original_text: Bank of America shall pay Supplier for all services and applicable taxes invoiced In arrears in accordance with the terms of this Agreement, within sixty (60) calendar days of the date of receipt of a valid and correct invoice by Bank of America. Bank of America reserves the right to pay prior to the expiration of the sixty (60) day period. If Bank of America pays within thirty (30) calendar days of receipt of a valid invoice by Bank of America, a discount of two percent (2%) will be subtracted from the total invoice amount for Services.
perturbation.changed_text: Bank of America shall pay Supplier for all services and applicable taxes invoiced In arrears in accordance with the terms of this Agreement, within ninety (90) calendar days of the date of receipt of a valid and correct invoice by Bank of America. Bank of America reserves the right to pay prior to the expiration of the ninety (90) day period. If Bank of America pays within sixty (60) calendar days of receipt of a valid invoice by Bank of America, a discount of one percent (1%) will be subtracted from the total invoice amount for Services.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to 90 days and reducing the discount can introduce conflict with prompt payment laws, especially if the supplier is a small business. The changed text introduces a conflict by extending the payment period beyond what is considered reasonable or legally permissible in certain jurisdictions, potentially harming the supplier's cash flow.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Prompt Payment Act generally requires federal agencies to pay invoices within 30 days. While this contract is not directly with a federal agency, extending payment terms to 90 days, especially with a reduced discount, can be viewed as an attempt to circumvent the principles of the Act, placing undue burden on the supplier.

Regulations (a) The Director of the Office of Management and Budget shall prescribe regulations to carry out section 3902 of this title . The regulations shall- (1) provide that the required payment date is- (A) the date payment is due under the contract for the item of property or service provided; or (B) except as provided in paragraphs (10) and (11), 30 days after a proper invoice for the amount due is received if a specific payment dat
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment terms to 90 days and reduces the discount for early payment, which contradicts the Prompt Payment Act's principles as explained in the Law Explanation. While the scraped law snippet (31 USC 3903) focuses on federal agencies and requires payment within 30 days, the LLM explanation notes that extending payment terms to 90 days can be seen as circumventing these principles, placing undue burden on the supplier. The 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition states that a legal contradiction arises when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal. Therefore, the changed payment terms introduce a potential legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Supplier shall deliver all Work in Progress relating to Bank of America Customizations to Bank of America within five (5) calendar days after the effective date of termination under Sections 5.1, 5.2, and 5.3 above. All right, title and interest in such Work in Progress relating to Bank of America Customizations (including copyright) shall be deemed assigned to and vested in Bank of America.
perturbation.changed_text: Supplier shall deliver all Work in Progress relating to Bank of America Customizations to Bank of America within two (2) calendar days after the effective date of termination under Sections 5.1, 5.2, and 5.3 above. All right, title and interest in such Work in Progress relating to Bank of America Customizations (including copyright) shall be deemed assigned to and vested in Bank of America.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the timeframe for delivering work in progress to 2 days may be insufficient and unreasonable, potentially conflicting with intellectual property law and contract law principles that require reasonable timeframes for fulfilling obligations. If the work in progress is substantial, 2 days might not allow enough time for a proper handover.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly a violation of UTSA, demanding handover in 2 days could be seen as unreasonable, especially if the supplier has trade secrets embedded in the 'work in progress.'  A court could find the timeframe insufficient to protect those trade secrets during the handover process, making it an unreasonable contractual obligation.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a delivery timeframe of five calendar days, while the changed text reduces this to two calendar days. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that this reduced timeframe may be insufficient and unreasonable, conflicting with contract law principles that require reasonable timeframes for fulfilling obligations. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that demanding handover in two days could be seen as unreasonable, especially if trade secrets are involved, potentially leading to an insufficient protection of those secrets during the handover process. While there are no law snippets provided, the combined explanations suggest that the reduced timeframe could be deemed unreasonable, potentially leading to a legal challenge based on the implied requirement of reasonableness in contract law and intellectual property considerations, satisfying the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: Bank of America may, at Its sole expense, perform a confidential audit of Supplier's operations as they pertain to the Products or services provided under this Agreement. Such audits shall be conducted on a mutually agreed upon date (which shall be no more than ten (10) Business Days after Bank of America's written notice of time, location and duration), subject to reasonable postponement by Supplier upon Supplier's reasonable request, provided, however, that no such postponement shall exceed twenty (20) Business Days.
perturbation.changed_text: Bank of America may, at Its sole expense, perform a confidential audit of Supplier's operations as they pertain to the Products or services provided under this Agreement. Such audits shall be conducted at Bank of America's sole discretion, with only two (2) Business Days' notice to the Supplier. The Supplier must comply with the audit and shall not postpone it for any reason.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for audits to two business days and prohibiting postponement may be deemed unreasonable and violate fair business practices, potentially creating grounds for legal challenge.  This especially affects smaller suppliers that might need more time to prepare.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing is part of contract law in most U.S. jurisdictions.  By allowing audits with minimal notice and no possibility of postponement, the modified clause arguably violates this covenant. It potentially prevents the supplier from adequately preparing, thereby undermining the fairness and reasonableness of the audit process.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as argued in the Law Explanation. The original text allowed for a mutually agreed-upon audit date with reasonable postponement options for the Supplier. The changed text mandates compliance with audits based on Bank of America's sole discretion with only two days' notice, precluding any postponement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'good faith' as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'observance of fair dealing standards'. By eliminating the supplier's ability to reasonably prepare for the audit, the modified clause arguably departs from the standard of 'fair dealing', thus creating a contradiction.
